summary: Gruber discusses two lingering habits from classic Mac OS that persist in OS X: reaching for the File menu to quit apps and using Cmd-N to create new folders instead of new Finder windows. He argues Apple was wrong to change the Cmd-N shortcut since millions of users had it deeply ingrained, while simultaneously advocating that users should generally adapt to OS X rather than fighting it with third-party hacks. He resolves the tension by distinguishing between stubbornly clinging to OS 9 behavior and simply needing a deeply wired keyboard shortcut, then provides an AppleScript hack to swap the shortcuts. The piece ends with the amusing observation that he immediately developed a new conflicting muscle memory for the same shortcut.
key_points: Apple was wrong to reassign Cmd-N in the Finder because established keyboard shortcuts become deeply ingrained muscle memory that shouldn't be changed lightly
Developers should carefully consider keyboard shortcuts from the outset, and if defaults must change, users should be able to reconfigure them
Users should generally embrace OS X on its own terms rather than using hacks to mimic OS 9 behavior — 'When in Rome, do as the Romans'
There's a meaningful distinction between stubbornly resisting change and needing to preserve a deeply wired keyboard shortcut
Muscle memory operates at different depths — years of Cmd-N for new folders is wired more deeply than months of Cmd-N for new windows
money_quotes: But they weren't starting from scratch. Millions of Mac users had been using Cmd-N to create new folders for years. This shouldn't even have been on the table.
The whole point of keyboard shortcuts is that you commit them to memory.
Mac OS X is offering you a round hole. Third party hacks will let you put your square peg inside, but it's not going to work as smoothly as using the round peg designed for the hole.
People in hell want ice water, but they're not going to get it.
It's a hack, but I consider it a clean hack, because it doesn't modify the Finder itself or any of its resources.
Cmd-N for making a new folder is wired into a much deeper part of my mind than it is for making a new window.
key_insight: Keyboard shortcuts become deeply ingrained muscle memory that developers should treat as a contract with users — changing them breaks trust and workflow, even when the new assignment is more logical.
